coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of magnetically reusable Fe3O4 nanospheres-N, S co-doped graphene quantum dots enclosed CdSe its application as a photocatalyst
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2019 The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Khataee, Alireza
Sajjadi, Saeed
Hasanzadeh, Aliyeh
Joo, Sang Woo
coredata.dc:description: Herein, Fe3O4 nanospheres attached on N, S co-doped graphene quantum dots (NSG) enclosed CdSe nanoparticles (CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4) is introduced as a magnetic high-performance photocatalyst for the heterogeneous photodegradation of methylene blue in aqueous solutions. The as-prepared CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 nanocatalyst showed high stability and adequate ferromagnetism which makes it easy to be separated and reused. CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 nanocomposites displayed enhanced photocatalytic activity for the degradation of organic compounds such as azo-dyes compared with CdSe nanoparticles. In view of these advantages offered by the prepared nanocatalyst, it has a high potential for the degradation of organic contaminants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cadmium selenide
Magnetic nanocatalyst
Nanocomposites
Graphene quantum dots
Photocatalytic degradation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and (b) high resolution TEM images of as-prepared CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 nanocomposites. SEM images of (c) CdSe, (d) CdSe/NSG, and (e) CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 prepared by hydrothermal reaction.

(a) XRD patterns of CdSe, CdSe/NSG, and CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 nanoparticles. The VSM hysteresis loops of (b) Fe3O4 and (c) CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 nanoparticles. (d) FT-IR spectra of CdSe and CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 nanoparticles.

(a) XPS survey spectrum of CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 nanocomposite; (b) core level XPS spectra of the O1s region; (c) core level XPS spectra of the C1s region. (d) (Ahυ)2 vs hυ plot of CdSe, CdSe/NSG, and CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 nanostructures. (e) The scheme of possible mechanism involved in the band gab of CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 nanocomposites.
BET plots of (a) CdSe and (b) CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 nanoparticles. (c) The absorption and (d) PL spectra of NSG QDs.

(a) Photocatalytic degradation of MB at room temperature under neutral pH. (b) Effect of different scavenger on the degradation efficiency of MB. (c) The cyclic operation of the as-prepared CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 nanocomposites for the degradation of MB.
The BET results of unmodified CdSe and CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 nanoparticles.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of magnetically reusable Fe3O4 nanospheres-N, S co-doped graphene quantum dots enclosed CdSe its application as a photocatalyst

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Laboratory of Advanced Water and Wastewater Treatment Processes, Department of Applied Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, 51666-16471 Tabriz, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Mechanical Engineering, Yeungnam University, Gyeongsan 712-749, South Korea.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Laboratory of Advanced Water and Wastewater Treatment Processes

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, Fe3O4 nanospheres attached on N, S co-doped graphene quantum dots (NSG) enclosed CdSe nanoparticles (CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4) is introduced as a magnetic high-performance photocatalyst for the heterogeneous photodegradation of methylene blue in aqueous solutions. The as-prepared CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 nanocatalyst showed high stability and adequate ferromagnetism which makes it easy to be separated and reused. CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 nanocomposites displayed enhanced photocatalytic activity for the degradation of organic compounds such as azo-dyes compared with CdSe nanoparticles. In view of these advantages offered by the prepared nanocatalyst, it has a high potential for the degradation of organic contaminants.

ce:section.ce:para: The environmental issues such as the increased discharge of dye wastewaters from various industries have become an important challenge due to their hazards to human and ecology resulted from poisonousness and non-biodegradability. On this basis, the treatment of contaminated water matrices has been the subject of the much systematic survey to reduce the risk related to it [1,2]. The discovery of photocatalytic water splitting on TiO2 electrodes in 1972 by Honda and Fujishima have led to an intensive search in the photocatalytic process to overcome the energy problem and environmental contamination [3]. The photocatalytic process of a semiconductor includes the creation of photo-induced electrons and holes in the conduction and valence bands, respectively and consequent chemical reactions with the nearby media after transferring the photo-excited charges to the powder surface where the organic compounds can be successfully disjointed [4,5]. The inadequate absorption in the visible spectrum and the skimp photonic efficiency of current photo-catalysts prevent the effective operation of low frequency light driven photocatalysis, so the application of this technology has become limited [6].

The Cadmium selenide (CdSe) is an n-type semiconductor with a bandgap energy in the range of 1.65–1.8 eV. CdSe nanoparticles are recognized as terrific photocatalyst due to the fast production of electron-hole pairs by light-excitation and the extremely negative reduction potentials of excited electrons [7]. Nevertheless, effective charge dissociation and narrowing of their band gap can enhance the photocatalytic degradation for various organic contaminants.

Recently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have appeared and turned into marvelous research attention as promising quantum dots (QDs). These zero-dimensional nanostructures consist of various sp2 hybridized hexagonal structure layers, which are less than 10 nm in size. Due to their remarkable low-toxicity, great biocompatibility, vigorous chemical inaction and optical properties, they have potential usages in photovoltaics, sensing, and bioimaging [8–10]. Furthermore, it has been indicated that doping carbon materials with heteroatoms is an efficient technique to adjust their band structures, which leading to the enhancement of photocatalytic activities. The interaction between catalyst and GQDs enhance the catalytic activity of catalyst through two possible reaction mechanisms. At first, because of the upconversion effect, the GQDs can absorb long wavelength lights and then emitting short wavelength UV photons. Next, the band of GQDs lets electron transfer from excited catalyst surface, endorsing charge separation and enhancing the amount of charge carriers available for reactions [11–13]. Tsai and Hsu investigated the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular (LUMO) levels of GQDs via cyclic-voltammetry assessments (+1.15 V and −2.6 V versus NHE, respectively) [14,15]. Herein, the CdSe/N, S co-doped GQDs (NSG) nanocomposites were suggested as a model heterojunction for low frequency light photocatalysis [16,17].

However, when CdSe particles are dispersed into the aquatic media, they adept to be lost and, thus making it hard to be recollected. To overcome this problem, photoactive CdSe particles are decorated with magnetic nanoparticles, and can be recollected by a magnetic field. Being magnetic, biocompatible and low-toxic, magnetite (Fe3O4) nanoparticles have possessed significant research interests [18,19].

In this study, N, S co-doped graphene quantum dots (NSG)-enclosed CdSe nanoparticles were prepared and anchored with Fe3O4 nanoparticles (CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4). The CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 nanocomposites were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), scanning electron microscope (SEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), vibrating-sample magnetometer (VSM), Fourier transformed infrared (FT-IR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), diffuse-reflectance spectrophotometer (DRS), Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) analysis, and photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL). Moreover, the photocatalytic activity of the as-prepared nanocatalyst was estimated by degradation of methylene blue (MB) under irradiation of UV light. Furthermore, the stability of this nanocatalyst was studied. The obtained results of this study demonstrated that the as-prepared nanocatalyst encompasses great revenue for the UV-light driven destruction of organic compounds. To the best of our knowledge, the photocatalytic activity of CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 nanocomposites has rarely been investigated.

All chemicals used in this study were of analytical grade and were used without further purification. Cadmium acetate dihydrate (Cd(CH3COO)2·2H2O 98%), hydrazine monohydrate (N2H4·H2O 80%), sodium selenite (Na2SeO3 99%) were purchased from Loba Chemie Co. (India). Citric acid (C6H8O7 99%), thiourea (CH4N2S 99%), iron(II) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl2·6H2O), iron(III) chloride hexa-hydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), and ammonia (NH3) were purchased from Aldrich. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) was purchased from Merck. Ethanol (97%) and MB were purchased from Daru Co. (Iran) and Shimi Boyakhsaz Co. (Iran), respectively. All chemicals were used without any purification, and distilled water was used for all experiments.

A hydrothermal method was used for the preparation of CdSe nanoparticles. 1 mmol of sodium hydroxide, cadmium acetate dehydrates, and sodium selenite were dissolved in 70 mL deionized water. At the same time, 1 mmol EDTA was dissolved in 20 mL deionized water and added to the prior solution. The gained solution stirred at high speed for 5 min, after that, hydrazine monohydrate (3 mL) was gradually added to the final solution. Eventually, the achieved solution moved into a 150 mL Teflon lined autoclave reactor and placed in a furnace at 200 °C for 24 h. The obtained black powders were gathered and purified with deionized water and ethanol for several times. Finally, the dying of the purified black powders is performed at room temperature for 12 h [20,21].

Once again, a hydrothermal method used for the preparation of NSG. For this purpose, citric acid (1 mmol) and thiourea (3 mmol) were completely dissolved in 70 mL deionized water. Then, the solution was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon lined autoclave reactor and placed in a furnace at 180 °C for 6 h. The obtained NSG were purified and gathered by adding ethanol and centrifuging at 5000 rpm for several times [14,22,23].

1 g of the as-prepared CdSe nanoparticles was firstly dispersed in 60 mL deionized water, then 25 mL of the as-prepared NSG (0.1 mg/mL) was added. The resulting admixture was sonicated for 1 h. After that, the sonicated admixture was transferred into a 150 mL Teflon lined autoclave reactor and placed in a furnace at 180 °C for 4 h. At last, the CdSe/NSG nanocomposites were purified by deionized water and ethanol. The as-prepared CdSe/NSG nanocomposites were dried in an oven at room temperature overnight [24].

Fe3O4 nanoparticles were obtained by the co-sedimentation of Fe2+ and Fe3+ with a molar ratio of 2:3 under the aqueous solution of ammonia as a sedimenting agent. In summary, iron(II) chloride hexahydrate (2.7 g) and iron(III) chloride hexahydrate (5.2 g) dissolved in 250 mL degassed deionized water. The obtained solution was kept at high-speed stirring and with the protection of Ar. 140 mL of ammonia solution was dropped gradually and the solution was kept stirring for 30 min. At last, the Fe3O4 nanoparticles were washed with deionized water and ethanol. The as-prepared Fe3O4 nanoparticles were collected by a magnet and dried at 60 °C for a short time [25].

1 g of the CdSe/NSG and 0.1 g of Fe3O4 were dispersed in 70 mL degassed deionized water by ultrasonic for 1 h. After that, the obtained mixture was moved into a 100 mL Teflon lined autoclave reactor and placed in a furnace at 180 °C for 24 h. The final products were washed with distilled water and ethanol for several times. After collecting by a magnet and drying at 60 °C overnight, the CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 nanocomposites were obtained [24].

The photocatalytic activity of the as-prepared CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 nanocomposites was assessed by degradation of aqueous solution of MB under UV-B irradiation. A 10 W UV lamp was used as a light source, and it was located in a dark box. All of the reactions were operated in a 250-mL crystallizer under magnetically stirring at room temperature. For the photocatalytic degradation of MB, 1 g/L of the CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 nanocomposites was added to the 100 mL MB (20 mg/L) aqueous solution. The MB solution was stirred in a dark box for 30 min to make the adsorption/desorption equilibrium among the CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 nanocomposites and MB molecules. After that, the mixture was illuminated with a UV-B lamp. At the regular time, aliquots volume was withdrawn and the CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 nanocomposites were separated by a magnet. The degradation of MB was measured by the UV–vis spectroscopy at λ = 660 nm [26,27]. The degradation efficiency (DE%) of MB was computed via the Eq. (1):
(1)
D E % =
1 -
C t
C 0
× 100
where Ct and Co are the concentration of MB dye (mg/L) at the beginning and the specified reaction time of t.

The morphology of the as-papered samples was studied with TEM (JEM-2200FS, JEOL, Japan) and SEM (Mira3 FEG-SEM Tescan, Czech Republic). The crystallographic features of the samples were examined with XRD (PANalyti-cal X’ Pert PRO, Germany). The magnetic properties of Fe3O4 and CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 samples were investigated by VSM (Meghnatis Kavir Kashan Co., Kashan, Iran). The FT-IR spectroscopy was carried out with a commercial IR-spectrometer (Tensor 27, Bruker, Germany) using the KBr pellet method. XPS evaluations of the as-papered samples were executed with a Thermo scientific photoelectron spectrometer ((K-Alpha, UK). DRS and BET analyses were performed with a diffuse reflectance spectrophotometer (S4100, Sinco Co., Korea) and a TriStar II Plus instrument using N2 adsorption/desorption at 77 K (Micromeritics, USA). The PL spectra and the quantum yield (QY) measurement of the as-papered NSG were accomplished by a PL spectrometer (Perkin Elmer LS45, USA).

TEM and SEM images used to investigate the successful loading of NSG on the surface of CdSe nanoparticles and the morphology of the as-prepared CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 nanocomposite. Fig. 1 shows the TEM (Fig. 1(a)) and high resolution TEM (Fig. 1(b)) images of CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4. As shown, the lattice spacing of 0.238, 0.159, and 0.28 nm corresponding to the (1120) plane of NSG, the (202) plane of CdSe, and the (311) plane of Fe3O4, respectively, which reflects the substantial deposition of Fe3O4 and GQDs on the surface of CdSe [28–30]. The SEM images in Fig. 1(c–e) presents CdSe, CdSe/NSG, and CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 nanoparticles. As shown in Fig. 1(c), the CdSe nanoparticles have agglomerated non-uniform shape and a diameter of about 40 nm. Nevertheless, for CdSe/NSG (Fig. 1(d)), the surface of the CdSe nanoparticles coated with NSG which caused to a fine dispersion and a uniform spherical-shaped of CdSe nanoparticles. Besides, Fig. 1(e) shows that Fe3O4 nanoparticles with about 10 nm diameter size were attached to the surface of CdSe/NSG nanoparticles [31]. The Fe3O4 nanoparticles attached on the surface of CdSe/NSG nanoparticles and CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 nanoparticles had diameters with a maximum abundance in the range of 8−12 nm and 50–70 nm, respectively (Fig. S1 in Supplementary material).

The elemental X-ray dot-mapping of O, Fe, Cd and Se for CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 nanoparticles are shown in Fig. S2 in Supplementary material. A uniform distribution of the elements can be observed, demonstrating the well-dispersion of both NSG and Fe3O4 throughout the CdSe nanoparticles.

The crystallinity of the CdSe, CdSe/NSG, and CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 nanoparticles was performed via the XRD analysis (Fig. 2 (a)). According to the main diffractogram peaks of prepared samples: (111), (220), and (311) for CdSe (JCPDS No. 65-2891) and (220), (311), (400), (422), (511) and (440) for Fe3O4 (JCPDS 19-0629), determined that the samples prepared by hydrothermal method belong to CdSe and Fe3O4 with cubic crystal structure [31,32]. Evidently, there is no variant peak and diffractogram peaks shift in CdSe/NSG compared with CdSe. This clearly indicates that the NSG, owing to its high dispersion and low crystallinity, has no significant impression on the crystal structure of CdSe [33]. Therefore, the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) graph (Fig. S3 in Supplementary material) of CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 are in good agreement with its XRD results.

The magnetic properties of Fe3O4 and CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 samples were investigated by the VSM. The saturation magnetization (Ms) of CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 sample was 29.75 emu/g, whereas for Fe3O4 Ms was 62.20 emu/g, chiefly owing to the enclosing of Fe3O4 on the surface of CdSe (Fig. 2(b and c)). The as-prepared CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 could be rapidly collected from aqueous solution by a magnet due to its significant Ms value. This would be helpful for CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 recycle and improving its productivity in practical applications [34,35].

The FT-IR spectra of CdSe and CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 nanoparticles displayed in Fig. 2(d). As shown in this figure, the existence of the absorption peaks around 764 cm−1 (CS stretch), 1231 cm−1 (CO stretch), 1294 cm−1 and 1361 cm−1 (CN stretch), and 1708 cm−1 (C = O stretch) indicates that the NSG structure contain sulfur and nitrile groups and successfully formed on the CdSe surface [36,37]. Moreover, it can be seen that the absorption peaks located around 532 cm−1, 1454 cm−1, and 3452 cm−1 are owing to the FeO vibration, OH stretching, and the bending vibration of adsorbed water respectively in CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 nanocomposite [38].

As depicted in Fig. 3 (a), the XPS spectrum of the CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 presents seven peaks at 54.67, 164.29, 287.9, 400.32, 406.33, 532.39, and 711.34 eV, which are related to Se3d, S2p, C1s, N1s, Cd3d, O1s, and Fe2p regions, respectively. This obviously illustrates that the GQDs prepared from citric acid and thiourea were doped with N and S atoms, and CdSe/NSG was successfully attached to Fe3O4. In the high-resolution spectra of O1s region, the peak at 530.08 eV is related to the FeO bond in Fe3O4 (Fig. 3(b)). In addition, the peak at 531.77 eV corresponds to the OH groups on the surface of CdSe/NSG, which is disclosing successful enclosing the CdSe by NSG. To recognize other contributions of the C1s (Fig. 3(c)) peak, the high-resolution spectra of its regions was investigated. As for the C1s spectra, the peaks at 284.2 eV and 284.66 eV are specified to the sp2 C and sp3 C in un-oxidized graphitic carbon lattice, while the subsequent peak at 285.15 eV is rather specified to the presence of some N-containing functional group in an organic matrix. Furthermore, the peaks located at 286.3 and 288.5 eV possibly derive from carbonyls and carboxylates functional groups in the sample [39,40].

It is extensively approved that the photocatalytic activity is closely related to the band gap of the photocatalyst. Hence, the band gap energy (Eg) of the CdSe, CdSe/NSG, and CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 nanostructures were measured via the Eq. (2) [41]:
(2)
(
A h υ )
2
=
K (
1240 λ
- E
g
)
where A (a constant value), hυ, and K are absorption factor, the photon energy (eV), and the autonomous constant. Eg was estimated from linear fitted areas of (Ahυ)2 vs hυ plot (Fig. 3(d)). The Eg of CdSe, CdSe/NSG, and CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 nanostructures were obtained to be 1.60, 1.22, and 1.16 eV respectively. Obviously, The Eg of CdSe was remarkably reduced with integrating NSG that could be ascribed to the effective charge transfer between the conduction and valence bands of CdSe and NSG. Also, the incorporation of Fe3O4 with CdSe/NSG decreased the Eg of the obtained nanostructured (CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4). Hence, the production of the electron-hole pair in semiconductors with short Eg takes place easier. The CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 nanocomposite showed better performance compared to pristine CdSe, NSG, and Fe3O4 samples for photocatalytic degradation of MB. Fig. 3(e) shows the scheme of possible mechanism involved in the band gab of CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 under light irradiation.

The BET analysis was done to evaluate the changes in the pore volume and specific surface area of the CdSe and CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 nanoparticles. N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms are indicated in Fig. 4 (a and b) and the results are given in Table 1 . The incorporation of NSG and Fe3O4 onto the CdSe surface enhanced the specific surface area of CdSe from 20.65 to 61.43 m2/g. Also, the pore volume was decreased and the pore sizes were changed from meso- to micro-size, which led to the improvement of the active sites on the surface of modified CdSe compared to CdSe nanoparticles.

The absorption spectra of NSG (Fig. 4(c)) displays four absorption bands at 360, 462, 579 and 596 nm, wherein the absorption peak at 360 nm attributed to n ≥ π* transition of the CO bond and the absorption peaks at 579 and 596 nm are relevant to the doping of sulfur. The peaks at 579 and 596 nm can be attributed to the π ≥ π*, and n ≥ π* of CS and SO. Besides, the NSG under excitation in the 305–425 nm region represent excitation independent PL (Fig. 4(d)). However, when the excitation wavelength goes over 405 nm, the PL become weak and excitation dependent, owing to the absorption peaks at 420–600 nm of sulfur doping [14,22,42]. Therefore, the excitation-independent PL behavior suggests that the PL properties of the NSG sample are depend on the surface states rather than the morphology. The NSG also display high PL quantum yield (68 %) under excitation of 345 nm. It is reasonable that the PL of the NSG should be attributed to the radiative recombination of electrons and holes trapped on the NSG surface. Consequently, it can be concluded that the strong PL emission of the NSG predominantly result from the surface-doped N and/or S atoms.

Herein, the possible processes included in MB degradation were surficial adsorption of MB molecules on the CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 in the nonexistence of UV-B irradiation, MB degradation under the UV-B irradiation and the nonexistence of any catalyst, and photocatalytic degradation of MB in the existence of CdSe, CdSe/NSG, and CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 under the UV-B irradiation, individually. The degradation performance of each process was evaluated and exhibited in Fig. 5 (a). As can be seen, there was no noticeable DE% through the surficial adsorption (10.34%) and UV-B irradiation (13.76%) which demonstrates the inadequacy of surficial adsorption and UV-B irradiation in removing of MB. The employment of CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 (93.67%) resulted higher DE% than that of found by CdSe/NSG (61.20%) and CdSe (42.18%) within 120 min, indicating the enhanced photocatalytic activity of CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 and CdSe/NSG in compared with CdSe. This enhancement in the photocatalytic degradation activity of CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 nanocomposite could be attribute to their broader light absorption region than unspoiled CdSe nanoparticles. Furthermore, the anchoring of NSG into CdSe nanoparticles could decrease the recombination of the photo-generated holes and electrons, resulting in improved photocatalytic degradation activity. For the further evaluation of the performance of the involved processes, the removal rate constants (kapp) of MB through above-mentioned processes were evaluated on the basis of pseudo-first order kinetic model. The obtained kapp for each of the involved process were presented in Fig. S4 in Supplementary material. As illustrate in Fig. S4, the highest kapp of 0.0514 min−1 was found for the degradation of MB by using the CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 nanocomposite. The kapp of the reaction with CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 nanocomposite was more than two and three folds that of the reaction with CdSe/NSG (0.0231 min−1) and CdSe (0.0158 min−1) nanostructures, respectively, therewith demonstrating that decoration of CdSe nanoparticles with NSG and Fe3O4 nanoparticles can enhance the photocatalytic degradation of MB.

For additional investigation, the photocatalytic activity of the as-prepared samples was estimated under the irradiation of visible light. Fig. S5 in Supplementary material shows the DE% of MB in the existence of CdSe, CdSe/NSG, and CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 under the irradiation of visible light. As demonstrated in Fig. S5, the DE% of MB in the existence of CdSe, CdSe/NSG, and CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 were found to be 63.18%, 45.67%, and 22.34%, respectively. These experimental results illustrate that CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 nanocomposite have fine photocatalytic activity even under the irradiation of visible light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of scavengers on the degradation of MB
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to determine the main factor in photocatalytic decomposition of MB molecules by CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 nanocomposite, a set of tests were carried out in the existence of various scavengers. The photocatalytic activity of CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 was investigated in the presence of 1, 4-benzoquinone and 2-propanol as
OOH/O2
¯ and
OH scavengers, and CrO3 and formic acid as e¯ and h+ scavengers, respectively [43–45]. As shown in Fig. 5(b), the DE% of MB was reduced in the existence of each of four scavengers. However, the reduction effects were most noticeable in the existence of h+ and
OH scavengers, which was confirming the assistance of h+ and
OH as the main oxidizing agents.

The reusability of the catalytic compounds for practically applicable catalysts is of high significance. The CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 catalyst could be easily separated from the MB aqueous solution by external magnetic field. Fig. 5(c) displays the graph comparing the ten recycles of degradation of MB solutions. The catalytic activity of CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 represented a little reduction after ten recycles. Moreover, DE% of CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 could reach up to 88% after 120 min, even if the CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 had been used for several times, which illustrates the desirable stability of CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 sample.

The comparison of surficial morphology and crystallinity of the CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 nanocomposite after the frequent tests are suggested as the usual techniques to evaluate the stability of the catalyst during the reactions. In this regard, SEM and XRD analyses were done on the CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 nanocomposite after ten repeated tests. The obtained SEM image and XRD pattern were represented in Figs. S6 and S7, respectively. Regarding the SEM image of the recycled CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 nanocomposite, the employment of CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 nanocomposite within sequential tests did not negatively affect its surficial morphology. Besides, the XRD pattern (Fig. S7) displayed the desirable crystallinity even after ten sequential tests.

In summary, a novel magnetic Fe3O4–NSG supported CdSe-sensitized were synthesized as an effective heterogeneous photocatalyst. The TEM images demonstrated their good dispersion, with diameters from 3 to 5 nm. The CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 nanocomposites were also characterized by SEM, XRD, VSM, FT-IR, XPS, DRS, BET and PL analyses. These outcomes illustrated that NSG deposited on CdSe could noticeably broaden the visible light absorption of CdSe and decreased the recombination of the photo-generated holes and electrons that improved the photocatalytic degradation activity of CdSe/NSG under UV–vis light irradiation (250–800 nm). Furthermore, the existence of Fe3O4 caused to the creation of a fine ferromagnetism which made the as-prepared photocatalyst easy to be separated and reused. The CdSe/NSG–Fe3O4 displayed the best photocatalytic activity, which was about two times higher than that of CdSe.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to thank the University of Tabriz (Iran) for the support provided. This work is funded by the grant NRF-2018R1A2B3001246 of the National Research Foundation of Korea.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jiec.2019.01.048.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
